beats.text: Here's one you haven't seen as an array today. 9 times 7 is 63. What's 7 times 9?

options: I'd need to see the array

validator.description: Student selected 63, correct application of property without visual support

beats.text: 63. You didn't need an array. You used the turn-around facts.
Now here's the big question. Will this work for ANY two numbers you multiply, not just the ones we tried?
Does 'multiply factors in any order and get the same product' work for ALL numbers?
options: Only when you have an array
Only when I know the first multiplication fact

validator.description: Student selected always works, correct generalization to universal principle

beats.text: Always. Any two numbers. You could build an array for 9 × 7 and rotate it. Same dots, same product. You can imagine an array even when you don't see one. That's why mathematicians call it a property, it's always true.

And here's something interesting, this isn't true for every operation. 8 minus 3 is NOT the same as 3 minus 8. But with multiplication, you can always switch the factors and get the same product. You already knew addition worked this way: 3 plus 5 equals 5 plus 3. Now you've proven multiplication works the same way.
